name: Eligibility and Entitlement
plainEnglish: Eligibility determines if a veteran is eligible to apply for Chapter 31 services based on discharge character and service-connection rating (10% or more). Entitlement is a separate decision by a Vocational Rehabilitation Counselor (VRC) determining whether the veteran needs services to prepare for, obtain, or retain suitable employment due to an employment handicap.
commonVaErrors: Informing veterans that eligibility automatically guarantees entitlement to services.
Telling veterans with a 10% rating that they are ineligible for VR&E (they are eligible, but require an SEH finding).
evidenceNeeded: VA Rating Decision showing 10% or higher service-connected disability.
DD-214 showing discharge character that is other than dishonorable.
Detailed resume showing history of employment barriers or aggravation of conditions.

name: Computer and Technology Supplies
plainEnglish: Under Chapter 31, VA may authorize 100% of approved, necessary books, supplies, equipment, software, and technology needed for the rehabilitation plan. A computer package is potentially covered under 38 C.F.R. § 21.212 when required by the school, necessitated by disability limitations, or required to prevent a distinct disadvantage.
commonVaErrors: Asserting there is a flat annual dollar cap (e.g. $500/year) on supplies or technology.
Denying a computer request without performing a written, individualized necessity analysis.
Conflating Chapter 33 Post-9/11 GI Bill book stipend limits ($1,000/year) with Chapter 31 VR&E uncapped supplies rules.
evidenceNeeded: Syllabus or course catalog listing specific computer/technology specifications.
School policy letter requiring computers for all enrolled students.
Medical/VRC documentation confirming a laptop/software is necessary to mitigate orthopedic, visual, or neurological disability limits.

name: Retroactive Induction
plainEnglish: If a veteran previously paid tuition or incurred educational expenses while holding a service-connected rating, and is subsequently found entitled to Chapter 31, the VA may retroactively authorize Chapter 31 benefits for that past training period under 38 C.F.R. § 21.282 and M28C.V.B.6.
commonVaErrors: Refusing to consider retroactive induction for training completed before the date of application.
Claiming retroactive induction is prohibited if the veteran used another VA benefit (like Post-9/11) during that period (dual benefit rules allow retroactive adjustment/re-election).
evidenceNeeded: Transcripts showing enrollment and grades for the retroactive period.
Itemized receipts or tuition invoices showing out-of-pocket payments or loans.
VA rating decision establishing service-connection existed during that study period.

name: Extensions Beyond 48 Months
plainEnglish: While standard entitlement is capped at 48 months, the VA may extend program duration under 38 U.S.C. § 3105 and 38 C.F.R. §§ 21.44 & 21.78 if determined necessary to overcome a Serious Employment Handicap (SEH). In 2024, VA updated delegation guidelines to allow VREOs to delegate extension concurrence authority.
commonVaErrors: Stating that the 48-month limit is a hard statutory cap that can never be extended.
Requiring VRCs to obtain personal VREO signature for extensions when authority was legally delegated under the 2024 final rule.
evidenceNeeded: Initial entitlement documentation showing a Serious Employment Handicap (SEH) finding.
IPE copy showing the current vocational objective is uncompleted.
Labor market data showing additional training is required to achieve suitable placement.

name: Independent Living Services
plainEnglish: For veterans whose service-connected disabilities make achievement of a vocational goal currently infeasible, the VA may authorize an Individualized Independent Living Program (IILP) under 38 U.S.C. § 3120 and 38 C.F.R. § 21.160. Services focus on daily living independence, assistive devices, and home modifications.
commonVaErrors: Denying independent living services solely because the veteran is capable of some sedentary work, without analyzing home-independence limitations.
Arbitrarily capping home adaption costs below statutory guidelines without specialized engineering surveys.
evidenceNeeded: Medical evaluations detailing severe limitations in activities of daily living.
Written determination from VRC confirming that vocational training is not currently feasible.

name: Reasonable Feasibility Rebuttal
plainEnglish: A VRC must determine if it is reasonably feasible for a veteran to achieve a vocational goal. Under 38 C.F.R. § 21.57, the VRC must resolve all doubts in favor of the veteran. If feasibility is denied, a veteran can submit rebuttals and request an Extended Evaluation.
commonVaErrors: Denying feasibility based on a checklist score, rather than a customized multidisciplinary assessment.
Resolving clinical or vocational ambiguities against the veteran (contrary to 38 C.F.R. § 21.57).
evidenceNeeded: Letters from private physicians stating that work is possible with accommodations.
References to successful employment or academic milestones achieved post-injury.
